---
title: "Lab 03: Principal Component Analysis"
---

::: {.lab-question}
**Fundamental question:** Which linear directions explain most of the variance in the data?
:::

## Short Intuition

PCA rotates the coordinate system so the first axis captures the largest variance, the second captures the largest remaining variance, and so on. With centered data, singular value decomposition gives these directions directly.

## Executable Code Block

```python
import torch

torch.manual_seed(11)

n = 120
latent = torch.randn(n, 2)
mix = torch.tensor([[2.0, 0.4, 0.2], [0.1, 0.9, 1.4]])
X = latent @ mix + 0.08 * torch.randn(n, 3)

X_centered = X - X.mean(dim=0, keepdim=True)
U, S, Vh = torch.linalg.svd(X_centered, full_matrices=False)

components = Vh[:2].T
X_2d = X_centered @ components
explained = (S**2) / (n - 1)
ratio = explained / explained.sum()

print("projected shape:", X_2d.shape)
print("components:")
print(components)
print("explained variance ratio:", ratio[:2])
```

## Hands-on Tasks & Solutions

1. Project the 3D dataset to one dimension and compute how much variance is retained.
2. Reconstruct the centered data from the 2D projection and measure mean squared reconstruction error.

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

```python
import torch

torch.manual_seed(11)

n = 120
latent = torch.randn(n, 2)
mix = torch.tensor([[2.0, 0.4, 0.2], [0.1, 0.9, 1.4]])
X = latent @ mix + 0.08 * torch.randn(n, 3)

Xc = X - X.mean(dim=0, keepdim=True)
_, S, Vh = torch.linalg.svd(Xc, full_matrices=False)

one_dim_ratio = (S[0] ** 2) / (S**2).sum()
Z = Xc @ Vh[:2].T
Xc_reconstructed = Z @ Vh[:2]
mse = torch.mean((Xc - Xc_reconstructed) ** 2)

print("one-dimensional retained variance:", one_dim_ratio.item())
print("2D reconstruction mse:", mse.item())
```

</details>
